quantum information is the result of reformulating
information theory in quantum mechanics ^QyhhPW4U

The state of a Quantum System ^qX8uBA5P

2-dimensional basis vectors ^YS1pf4Q3

complex vector for general states ^aIWSk5GO

State Space Postulate
The state of a system is described by a unit
vector in a Hilbert space ^3Ly4AsTJ

depending on the degree of freedom of the system
being considered,    may be infinite-dimensional ^T4r1OM4t

for describing realistic models of quantum
computation, we will be interested in degrees of
freedom for which the state is described by a
vector in a finite-dimensional (complex) Hilbert
space ^XLi881sU

two-level systems ^grpBN5fK

the state of each two-level system is described
by a vector in a 2-dimensional Hilbert space ^XqlLEDh3

the condition that the state is described by a
unit vector means that ^2sXKWYdx

normalization constraint ^z1tqevn2

the general state of the system is a superposition
of both possible states ^NqtiAAcr

a quantum mechanical two-level system ^ndgjn60C

a single photon that can be found in one of two
distinct paths ^WJTCoc3a

the presence or absence of a photon in a
particular path or location ^pj6COiu2

convenient basis ^3HuIRKzD

a unit vector for the state in which a photon
is not present ^mcYDjZW3

an orthogonal unit vector for the state in which
a photon is present ^RZ8oGJ5j

basis vectors: ^Kfgm5iPB

computational basis ^nWYlyeLd

the general state of the system can be expressed
by the vector ^1H9nUYCt

complex coefficients ^HApzDvhl

amplitudes ^mVCPvJzu

a complex amplitude    can be
decomposed unique as a
product ^ujyYORoT

where     is the non-negative
real number corresponding to
the magnitude of    and
              has norm 1 ^ccXXzpez

is known as the 'phase' ^llXhZoyE

is the 'phase factor' ^wXVJNThu

particles have a degree of

the state described by the vector         is 
equivalent to the state described by the vector

on the other hand, relative phase factors between

Measurement ^7wkgEHxn

Evolution Postulate ^argrS132

assumes that the quantum system is closed ^ZZhNejh2

is not allowed to interact with its environment ^MwTpTrIB

need to measure ^H8ra5S6u

allow the system to interact with the measurement
apparatus of some observer ^NkMRTr7N

the original system is no longer closed ^FkGLX3L1

the Evolution Postulate is no longer appropriate
for describing its evolution ^Ou49PFio

the evolution of the state of a system during a
measurement process is not unitary ^RzgZN3O0

if the state               is provided as input ^FOYOL1mU

it will output label   with probability ^b99YNvB9

and leave the system in state ^JlWPVYfa

Measurement Postulate ^YHClIqgd

for a given orthonormal basis                  of a
state space       for a system A, it is possible
to perform a Von Neumann measurement on
system     with respect to the basis B that,
given a state ^u4w5FQ00

outputs a label   with probability        and
leaves the system in state ^gDK1ZfvC

furthermore, given a state ^8LZuexL8

from a bipartite state space              ( the
are orthonormal; the      have unit norm but are
not necessarily orthogonal), then performing a
Von Neumann measurement on system A will yield
outcome   with probability        and leave the
bipartite system in state ^OJ1hpmGL

for the state ^wa8pLTye

the statistics of any
measurement are exactly
the same ^DyCF0vqc

global phases have no
physical significance ^g43xlYAB

the states       and           are equal ^cWj3ZbdA

differing only by a global phase ^zdBiLhEY

same probability ^U3o8Od9z

first qubit ^yeV4FYMv

second qubit ^Ra7iXcyp

to see what happens if we just measure the first

rewrite       as ^5yL3GIyS

an orthogonal projection is an operator P ^4kuOzeGg

Von Neumann measurement ^V1eMZuTA

special kind of projective measurement ^NRRNbMIi

for any decomposition of the identity operator
              into orthogonal projectors    , there
exists a projective measurement that outputs
outcome   with probability                       and
leaves the system in the renormalizes state ^EC4UmSRA

this measurement projects the input state     
into one of the orthogonal subspaces
corresponding to the projection operators     ^Nx1eJuPj

special case of a projective measurement where
all of the projectors    have rank one ^NBw7qMIv

often described in terms of an observable ^UZc5uZ9F

Hermitean operator M acting on the
state space of the system ^7wY5wakM

measuring an observable corresponds to performing
a projective measurement with respect to the
decomposition               where the measurement
outcome   corresponds to the eigenvalue  ^2aNozdTo
